title: PNG: Lossless Compression with Transparency
concept: PNG is a lossless format that shrinks files without discarding any pixel data, and it uniquely supports an alpha channel for transparency, making it the standard choice for graphics, icons, and screenshots.

exercises.prompt: Recap: what causes blocky JPEG artifacts to appear in a photo?

options.text: Heavy lossy compression discarding fine detail inside pixel blocks
The photo being stored as vector shapes instead of pixels
Saving the file only a single time at low compression

exercises.explain: Right — heavy JPEG compression discards detail inside blocks, and that shows up as visible artifacts.

cards.title: The logo that floats on any background
cards.body: Notice how a website logo can sit cleanly on a white header, a dark footer, or a photo background, with no ugly white box around it? That's not magic — it's a file format that can mark some pixels as "see-through." JPEG can't do that. PNG can.

cards.title: What makes PNG different
cards.body: PNG (Portable Network Graphics) is a lossless image format — it compresses files without discarding any pixel data, so decompressing gives back the exact original image. PNG also supports an alpha channel: an extra value per pixel that controls how transparent or opaque it is.

cards.title: A pane of frosted glass
cards.body: Think of a normal image as an opaque painting — every pixel is a solid color. PNG's alpha channel is like painting on glass instead of canvas: some areas stay fully solid, some are fully see-through, and some are anywhere in between, like frosted glass.

cards.title: Screenshots and app icons
cards.body: A screenshot of a text-heavy webpage is usually saved as PNG, because lossless compression keeps sharp text edges crisp with no JPEG-style blur or artifacts. An app icon with rounded corners is also PNG, so the corners outside the rounded shape can be transparent instead of an ugly white square.

cards.title: "PNG is always better than JPEG"
cards.body: PNG isn't universally superior — it's a tradeoff. Because it can't discard detail the way JPEG does, a PNG of a complex photo with millions of subtle colors is usually much larger than a JPEG of the same photo. PNG wins for graphics, text, and transparency; JPEG usually wins for photographs.

cards.title: PNG vs. JPEG
cards.body: When each format tends to be the better fit:

left.text:
Lossless, no detail lost
Supports transparency (alpha)
Best for logos, icons, screenshots

right.text:
Lossy, some detail lost
No transparency support
Best for photographs

exercises.prompt: What does PNG's alpha channel let a pixel do that a standard JPEG pixel can't?

options.text: Be partially or fully transparent instead of a fixed solid color

exercises.explain: Correct — the alpha channel adds a transparency value per pixel, something JPEG has no way to represent.

exercises.prompt: True or false: PNG compression discards some image detail, similar to JPEG.
exercises.explain: False. PNG is lossless — decompressing a PNG restores the exact original pixel data with nothing discarded.

exercises.sentence: PNG's extra per-pixel transparency value is called the ___ channel.
exercises.explain: Alpha is correct — the alpha channel controls how transparent or opaque each pixel is.

exercises.prompt: A designer needs an icon that sits cleanly on top of different colored backgrounds with no visible box around it. Which format should they export it as?

options.text: PNG — its alpha channel supports transparent pixels
JPEG — it compresses images the smallest
Either format works identically for this

exercises.explain: PNG is correct — only its alpha channel lets the background show through where the icon doesn't cover it.

exercises.prompt: A team is building a webpage. They need a hero photograph and a logo that must sit transparently over different colored sections. Which format should each use, and why?

options.text: JPEG for the photograph (smaller file, complex colors), PNG for the logo (lossless, supports transparency)
PNG for both, since PNG is always the smaller file regardless of content
JPEG for both, since JPEG supports transparency just as well as PNG
PNG for the photograph and JPEG for the logo

exercises.explain: Exactly. JPEG's lossy compression keeps the complex photo's file size small; PNG's lossless, transparency-capable format keeps the logo crisp and background-independent. Next: putting it all together — how to actually choose the right file format for a given job.
